lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: Gregory, o’ my word, we’ll not carry coals.

lines.speaker: GREGORY
lines.text: No, for then we should be colliers.

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: I mean, an we be in choler, we’ll draw.

lines.speaker: GREGORY
lines.text: Ay, while you live, draw your neck out o’ the collar.

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: I strike quickly, being moved.

lines.speaker: GREGORY
lines.text: But thou art not quickly moved to strike.

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: A dog of the house of Montague moves me.

lines.speaker: GREGORY
lines.text: To move is to stir; and to be valiant is to stand:

lines.speaker: GREGORY
lines.text: therefore, if thou art moved, thou runn’st away.

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: A dog of that house shall move me to stand: I will

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: take the wall of any man or maid of Montague’s.

lines.speaker: GREGORY
lines.text: That shows thee a weak slave; for the weakest goes to the wall.

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: True; and therefore women, being the weaker vessels, are ever thrust to

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: the wall: therefore I will push Montague’s men from the wall, and

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: thrust his maids to the wall.

lines.speaker: GREGORY
lines.text: The quarrel is between our masters and us their men.

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: ’Tis all one, I will show myself a tyrant: when I have

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: fought with the men, I will be cruel with the maids, and

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: cut off their heads.

lines.speaker: GREGORY
lines.text: The heads of the maids?

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: Ay, the heads of the maids, or their maidenheads; take it in

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: what sense thou wilt.

lines.speaker: GREGORY
lines.text: They must take it in sense that feel it.

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: Me they shall feel while I am able to stand: and ’tis

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: known I am a pretty piece of flesh.

lines.speaker: GREGORY
lines.text: ’Tis well thou art not fish; if thou hadst, thou hadst been

lines.speaker: GREGORY
lines.text: poor John. Draw thy tool; here comes two of the house of

lines.speaker: GREGORY
lines.text: the Montagues.

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: My naked weapon is out: quarrel, I will back thee.

lines.speaker: GREGORY
lines.text: How! turn thy back and run?

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: Fear me not.

lines.speaker: GREGORY
lines.text: No, marry; I fear thee!

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: Let us take the law of our sides; let them begin.

lines.speaker: GREGORY
lines.text: I will frown as I pass by, and let them take it as they list.

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: Nay, as they dare. I will bite my thumb at them; which

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: is a disgrace to them, if they bear it.

lines.speaker: ABRAHAM
lines.text: Do you bite your thumb at us, sir?

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: I do bite my thumb, sir.

lines.speaker: ABRAHAM
lines.text: Do you bite your thumb at us, sir?

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: Is the law of our side, if I say ay?

lines.speaker: GREGORY
lines.text: No.

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: No, sir, I do not bite my thumb at you, sir, but

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: I bite my thumb, sir.

lines.speaker: GREGORY
lines.text: Do you quarrel, sir?

lines.speaker: ABRAHAM
lines.text: Quarrel, sir! no, sir.

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: If you do, sir, I am for you: I serve as good

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: a man as you.

lines.speaker: ABRAHAM
lines.text: No better.

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: Well, sir.

lines.speaker: GREGORY
lines.text: Say “better:” here comes one of my master’s kinsmen.

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: Yes, better, sir.

lines.speaker: ABRAHAM
lines.text: You lie.

lines.speaker: SAMPSON
lines.text: Draw, if you be men. Gregory, remember thy swashing blow. They fight.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Part, fools!

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Put up your swords; you know not what you do. Beats down their swords.

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: What, art thou drawn among these heartless hinds?

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: Turn thee, Benvolio, look upon thy death.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: I do but keep the peace: put up thy sword,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Or manage it to part these men with me.

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: What, drawn, and talk of peace! I hate the word,

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: As I hate hell, all Montagues, and thee:

lines.speaker: TYBALT
lines.text: Have at thee, coward! They fight.

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: Clubs, bills, and partisans! strike! beat them down!

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: Down with the Capulets! down with the Montagues!

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: What noise is this? Give me my long sword, ho!

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: A crutch, a crutch! why call you for a sword?

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: My sword, I say! Old Montague is come,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: And flourishes his blade in spite of me.

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: Thou villain Capulet—Hold me not, let me go.

lines.speaker: LADY MONTAGUE
lines.text: Thou shalt not stir a foot to seek a foe.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Rebellious subjects, enemies to peace,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Profaners of this neighbour-stained steel—

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Will they not hear? What, ho! you men, you beasts,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: That quench the fire of your pernicious rage

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: With purple fountains issuing from your veins,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: On pain of torture, from those bloody hands

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Throw your mistemper’d weapons to the ground,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: And hear the sentence of your moved prince.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Three civil brawls, bred of an airy word,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: By thee, old Capulet, and Montague,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Have thrice disturb’d the quiet of our streets,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: And made Verona’s ancient citizens

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Cast by their grave beseeming ornaments,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: To wield old partisans, in hands as old,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Canker’d with peace, to part your canker’d hate:

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: If ever you disturb our streets again,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Your lives shall pay the forfeit of the peace.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: For this time, all the rest depart away:

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: You, Capulet, shall go along with me:

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: And, Montague, come you this afternoon,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: To know our further pleasure in this case,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: To old Free-town, our common judgment-place.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Once more, on pain of death, all men depart.

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: Who set this ancient quarrel new abroach?

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: Speak, nephew, were you by when it began?

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Here were the servants of your adversary,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: And yours, close fighting ere I did approach:

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: I drew to part them: in the instant came

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: The fiery Tybalt, with his sword prepared,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Which, as he breathed defiance to my ears,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: He swung about his head and cut the winds,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Who nothing hurt withal hiss’d him in scorn:

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: While we were interchanging thrusts and blows,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Came more and more and fought on part and part.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Till the prince came, who parted either part.

lines.speaker: LADY MONTAGUE
lines.text: O, where is Romeo? saw you him to-day?

lines.speaker: LADY MONTAGUE
lines.text: Right glad I am he was not at this fray.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Madam, an hour before the worshipp’d sun

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Peer’d forth the golden window of the east,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: A troubled mind drave me to walk abroad;

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Where, underneath the grove of sycamore

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: That westward rooteth from the city’s side,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: So early walking did I see your son:

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Towards him I made, but he was ware of me

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: And stole into the covert of the wood:

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: I, measuring his affections by my own,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: That most are busied when they’re most alone,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Pursued my humour not pursuing his,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: And gladly shunn’d who gladly fled from me.

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: Many a morning hath he there been seen,

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: With tears augmenting the fresh morning’s dew,

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: Adding to clouds more clouds with his deep sighs;

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: But all so soon as the all-cheering sun

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: Should in the furthest east begin to draw

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: The shady curtains from Aurora’s bed,

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: Away from light steals home my heavy son,

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: And private in his chamber pens himself,

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: Shuts up his windows, locks fair daylight out

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: And makes himself an artificial night:

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: Black and portentous must this humour prove,

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: Unless good counsel may the cause remove.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: My noble uncle, do you know the cause?

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: I neither know it nor can learn of him.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Have you importuned him by any means?

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: Both by myself and many other friends:

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: But he, his own affections’ counsellor,

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: Is to himself—I will not say how true—

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: But to himself so secret and so close,

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: So far from sounding and discovery,

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: As is the bird bit with an envious worm,

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: Ere he can spread his sweet leaves to the air,

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: Or dedicate his beauty to the sun.

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: Could we but learn from whence his sorrows grow,

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: We would as willingly give cure as know.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: See, where he comes: so please you, step aside;

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: I’ll know his grievance, or be much denied.

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: I would thou wert so happy by thy stay,

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: To hear true shrift. Come, madam, let’s away.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Good morrow, cousin.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Is the day so young?

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: But new struck nine.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Ay me! sad hours seem long.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Was that my father that went hence so fast?

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: It was. What sadness lengthens Romeo’s hours?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Not having that, which, having, makes them short.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: In love?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Out—

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Of love?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Out of her favour, where I am in love.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Alas, that love, so gentle in his view,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Should be so tyrannous and rough in proof!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Alas, that love, whose view is muffled still,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Should, without eyes, see pathways to his will!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Where shall we dine? O me! What fray was here?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Yet tell me not, for I have heard it all.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Here’s much to do with hate, but more with love.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Why, then, O brawling love! O loving hate!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: O anything, of nothing first create!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: O heavy lightness! serious vanity!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Mis-shapen chaos of well-seeming forms!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Feather of lead, bright smoke, cold fire, sick health!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Still-waking sleep, that is not what it is!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: This love feel I, that feel no love in this.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Dost thou not laugh?

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: No, coz, I rather weep.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Good heart, at what?

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: At thy good heart’s oppression.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Why, such is love’s transgression.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Griefs of mine own lie heavy in my breast,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Which thou wilt propagate, to have it prest

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: With more of thine: this love that thou hast shown

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Doth add more grief to too much of mine own.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Love is a smoke raised with the fume of sighs;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Being purged, a fire sparkling in lovers’ eyes;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Being vex’d, a sea nourish’d with lovers’ tears:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: What is it else? a madness most discreet,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: A choking gall and a preserving sweet.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Farewell, my coz.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Soft! I will go along;

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: An if you leave me so, you do me wrong.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Tut, I have lost myself; I am not here;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: This is not Romeo, he’s some other where.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Tell me in sadness, who is that you love.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: What, shall I groan and tell thee?

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Groan! why, no;

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: But sadly tell me who.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Bid a sick man in sadness make his will:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Ah, word ill urged to one that is so ill!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: In sadness, cousin, I do love a woman.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: I aim’d so near, when I supposed you loved.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: A right good mark-man! And she’s fair I love.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: A right fair mark, fair coz, is soonest hit.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Well, in that hit you miss: she’ll not be hit

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: With Cupid’s arrow; she hath Dian’s wit;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And, in strong proof of chastity well arm’d,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: From love’s weak childish bow she lives unharm’d.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: She will not stay the siege of loving terms,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Nor bide the encounter of assailing eyes,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Nor ope her lap to saint-seducing gold:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: O, she is rich in beauty, only poor,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: That when she dies with beauty dies her store.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Then she hath sworn that she will still live chaste?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: She hath, and in that sparing makes huge waste,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: For beauty starved with her severity

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Cuts beauty off from all posterity.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: She is too fair, too wise, wisely too fair,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: To merit bliss by making me despair:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: She hath forsworn to love, and in that vow

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Do I live dead that live to tell it now.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Be ruled by me, forget to think of her.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: O, teach me how I should forget to think.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: By giving liberty unto thine eyes;

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Examine other beauties.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: ’Tis the way

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: To call hers exquisite, in question more:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: These happy masks that kiss fair ladies’ brows

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Being black put us in mind they hide the fair;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: He that is strucken blind cannot forget

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: The precious treasure of his eyesight lost:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Show me a mistress that is passing fair,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: What doth her beauty serve, but as a note

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Where I may read who pass’d that passing fair?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Farewell: thou canst not teach me to forget.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: I’ll pay that doctrine, or else die in debt.

stage_directions.text: Enter Sampson and Gregory, of the house of Capulet, armed with swords and bucklers.
Enter several of both houses, who join the fray; then enter Citizens, with clubs.
Enter Capulet in his gown, and Lady Capulet.
Exeunt all but Montague, Lady Montague, and Benvolio.

glosses.definition: endure insult; do menial work — to 'carry coals' was proverbially demeaning
coal-carriers — also slang for cheats or dirty fellows
anger; one of the four humors, the hot bile
the hangman's noose — Sampson's pun on choler/collier/collar
provoked to anger
claim the cleaner inner-street position, forcing others into the gutter — a gesture of dominance
the weak are pushed aside — proverb turned on its head by Sampson
virginities — Sampson's crude sexual bragging
dried, salted hake — a cheap food associated with impotence
small round shields used with short swords
mild oath, from 'by the Virgin Mary' — roughly 'indeed'
an insulting gesture made by flicking the thumbnail from behind the upper teeth
a cause for fighting; a pretext
a slashing, swaggering sword-stroke
cowardly peasants — 'hinds' = servants, with a pun on 'female deer'
base-born man; peasant — not yet the modern sense of 'criminal'
an old-fashioned heavy weapon; the elder Capulet calls for one contemptuously, age-inappropriate
defilers; those who desecrate sacred things
swords stained with neighbors' blood
ill-tempered; used in anger (also: poorly forged)
angered prince
trivial comment; mere breath
the elder townsmen of Verona
dignified ceremonial garments of old age
pay the penalty
Villafranca, Escalus's judgment-seat near Verona
started; set in motion (like tapping a wine-cask)
with it; therewith
fight; brawl
drove (archaic past tense)
mood; temperament governed by the four bodily humors
the name puns on 'sick-amour' — sick love
hiding-place; thicket
Roman goddess of the dawn
sad son — 'heavy' means melancholy
ominous; warning of evil
confession
unloved by her; out of the favor of the lady he loves

glosses.phrase: Alas, that love, so gentle in his view
glosses.definition: Romeo begins his Petrarchan-lover complaint, heaping paradoxes on Cupid
blindfolded — Cupid was traditionally depicted blind
quarrelsome love — the first of Romeo's stacked oxymorons
hateful love — the play's central oxymoronic pairing
formless disorder — from which creation was said to proceed
weightless heaviness — impossible image
visible emptiness — paradox
passionless flame — Petrarchan cliché
unhealthy well-being — oxymoron
wakeful sleep; restless unconsciousness
increase; multiply
vapor of sighs — love-sickness was thought to produce literal breath-clouds
prudent; judicious
wait; stop — exclamation of interruption
in earnest; seriously
guessed so close to the truth
actually in love
a good archer — continuing the Cupid-as-archer image
attractive target
a very beautiful target
the wisdom of Diana — Roman goddess of the moon, hunt, and chastity

glosses.phrase: strong proof of chastity well arm’d
glosses.definition: heavily armored by virginity; immune to desire
Cupid's boyish weapon — dismissed by Rosaline's chastity
the seductive gaze of suitors
courtship as military campaign — a Petrarchan commonplace
open
only poor in that all her beauty will die with her — Rosaline has vowed chastity
self-denying; abstinent
sworn off love
Romeo's sighs swell the clouds; Petrarchan weather-metaphor
in earnest; seriously (Benvolio pressing Romeo)
descendants; future generations — Rosaline will leave no heir

annotations.title: Sampson and Gregory — beginning in prose and bawdry
annotations.body: The play opens not with the noble lovers but with two Capulet servants trading puns and sexual insults in prose. This is a deliberate structural choice. Shakespeare's Verona is a city whose servants also carry the feud — violence is stitched into the social fabric from the bottom up. The bawdry ('maidenheads,' 'pretty piece of flesh,' 'take the wall') establishes that the feud is about masculine display, territory, and sexual honour as much as about high-family principle. Notice how rapidly the register shifts: prose for servants, verse for Benvolio and Tybalt, blank verse for the Prince. The play's verse/prose ecology mirrors its social hierarchy and makes possible the Nurse's later prose among the nobles' verse — a stylistic marker of her class and comic function.
sources: Russ McDonald, Shakespeare and the Arts of Language (2001)

annotations.title: The Prince's first entry — civic peace as precondition
annotations.body: Escalus arrives to break up the third street-fight in the play's opening minutes and declares that any future breach will be a capital offence. His speech ('Rebellious subjects, enemies to peace, / Profaners of this neighbour-stained steel') is written in commanding blank verse and establishes a legal frame that will prove decisive: Romeo's killing of Tybalt in 3.1 is not punishable by death only because Tybalt had already killed Mercutio — the Prince's later decision of exile rather than execution is the compromise that launches the tragedy's second half. The Prince appears only three times in the play (1.1, 3.1, 5.3), forming a structural spine: establishing the law, adjudicating the first major breach, and delivering the final verdict.
sources: Stephen Greenblatt, Will in the World (2004)

annotations.title: Romeo's Petrarchan complaint — love as performance
annotations.body: Romeo's first long speech is a study in Petrarchan affectation. 'Feather of lead, bright smoke, cold fire, sick health' — these stacked oxymorons are not Romeo's private anguish but a public performance of the sixteenth-century lover's script, inherited from Francesco Petrarca's sonnets to Laura. Elizabethan sonneteers (Sidney, Spenser, Daniel) had made the Petrarchan complaint a fashionable literary posture. Shakespeare lets Romeo perform it at exhaustive length so that we hear, when he meets Juliet in 1.5, how radically his language shifts: at the ball he speaks in the sonnet form itself, not in the sonnet's clichés about it. The play is arguing something about language and love: the Petrarchan mode is a pose; genuine love requires finding a different syntax.
sources: Jill L. Levenson, The Oxford Shakespeare: Romeo and Juliet (2000)

trials.prompt: Why does the play open with servants rather than the titular lovers?
options: To provide comic relief before the tragedy
To show the feud reaches all levels of Veronese society, not just the noble houses
To introduce minor characters who will die later
Standard opening convention of Elizabethan drama required it

trials.prompt: What rhetorical figure dominates Romeo's first long speech about Rosaline ('cold fire,' 'bright smoke,' 'sick health')?
options: Anaphora (repetition of opening words)
Oxymoron — stacked paradoxical pairings characteristic of Petrarchan love-poetry
Metonymy
Synecdoche

trials.prompt: What critical view does Shakespeare take on Romeo's Rosaline-talk in 1.1?
options: He endorses it as genuine feeling
He treats it neutrally without commentary
He frames it as performative and affected — a pose borrowed from Petrarchan sonnet convention, to be contrasted with the real love in 1.5
He mocks it as universally insincere

trials.prompt: The Prince's decree that 'if ever you disturb our streets again, / Your lives shall pay the forfeit of the peace' is structurally important because —
options: It has no real consequence later in the play
It sets the legal stake that Romeo's later killing of Tybalt will activate, shaping his punishment
It is immediately forgotten by all characters
It only applies to commoners, not the nobility

trials.prompt: What is Benvolio's first action when he arrives at the Capulet-Montague brawl?
options: He encourages the fighting
He draws his sword and tries to part the combatants, urging 'Part, fools!'
He runs to fetch the Prince
He stays hidden and watches
